question: What causes jank in Flutter and how do you optimize performance?
answer:
Jank = dropped frames (< 60fps / >16ms per frame). The UI thread must complete layout+paint in 16ms.

Common jank causes:
1. Heavy computation on the UI thread (should use isolates)
2. Excessive widget rebuilds (setState rebuilds too much)
3. Large images not sized/cached properly
4. Missing const constructors
5. Synchronous I/O
6. Memory pressure causing GC pauses

Optimization tools:
• Flutter DevTools → Performance tab, Rebuild counts
• Flutter Inspector → Highlight repaints
• --profile mode: flutter run --profile
• Timeline events, flame charts

question: How do you detect and fix memory leaks in Flutter?
answer:
Common sources of memory leaks in Flutter:

1. StreamSubscription not cancelled in dispose()
2. AnimationController not disposed
3. TextEditingController / FocusNode not disposed
4. Timer not cancelled
5. GlobalKey held in static variables
6. Closures capturing large objects
7. Image cache growing unbounded

Detection tools:
• Flutter DevTools → Memory tab → heap snapshots
• Leak Tracker package (flutter_test)
• Observatory → allocation profiling

question: How do you profile a Flutter app and read DevTools timeline correctly?
answer:
Performance work without measurement is folklore. Always start by profiling, never by guessing.

The rules:
• Always profile in `--profile` mode. Debug mode is up to 10x slower for irrelevant reasons (assertions, no AOT) and `--release` strips the timeline.
• Run on a real low-end device when possible — your iPhone 15 Pro hides problems your users on a 2018 Android will hit.
• Reproduce a steady scenario (scroll the same list, replay the same animation) so the timeline is comparable across runs.

DevTools tabs and what they tell you:
• Performance — frame timeline. Green = under budget, yellow/red = jank. Click a frame to see the build/layout/paint/raster cost
• CPU profiler — flame chart of where Dart code spent time. Wide bars are hot functions; sample at high frequency for short events.
• Memory — heap snapshots, GC pauses. A growing heap between identical actions usually means a leak.
• Inspector → "Highlight Repaints" — shows which layers actually repainted; surprising repaints point to missing RepaintBoundary or non-const subtrees.
• Inspector → "Show Performance Overlay" — two stacked bars (UI / GPU) on the running app, frame-by-frame.

Reading the timeline:
• A red frame on UI bar = build/layout took too long → check the CPU profile for that frame
• A red frame on GPU bar but green UI = paint/raster too expensive → check for backdrop filters, large images, opacity layers
• Pattern of intermittent yellow = GC; check Memory tab for allocation rate

question: How do you cut unnecessary rebuilds — const, selector pattern, RepaintBoundary?
answer:
Most jank in Flutter apps is caused by widgets rebuilding when they didn't need to. Three tools, each at a different level, do most of the heavy lifting.

1) const constructors:
• A widget annotated `const` with const-constructible arguments is identity-compared by Flutter — if the same const reaches the same slot twice, the framework skips the entire rebuild of that subtree
• Lint `prefer_const_constructors` and `prefer_const_constructors_in_immutables` catches the easy cases — turn them on

2) Fine-grained reads (selector pattern):
• Watching a coarse store rebuilds the consumer on any change. With Riverpod, use `ref.watch(provider.select((s) => s.field))`. With Provider, use `Selector<T, Sel>`. With BLoC, use `BlocSelector` or `buildWhen`.
• If your selector doesn't help, the underlying state is too coarse — split it into focused providers/cubits

3) RepaintBoundary:
• Different from rebuild: a repaint boundary forces Flutter to allocate a separate compositing layer. If a sibling animates and your subtree is expensive to paint, putting your subtree under a RepaintBoundary stops it from being repainted just because the sibling changed.
• Don't sprinkle it everywhere — each boundary costs memory. Add one only at known-expensive boundaries with measured wins.

Other high-leverage moves:
• Lift `setState` as low in the tree as possible — only the StatefulWidget rebuilds, not its parents
• Avoid creating new closures or new objects in `build()` — extract to fields or `final` locals where possible
• ListView/GridView.builder, not ListView with a children list, for any list bigger than what fits on screen

question: How do you optimize image rendering — sizing, caching, and codecs?
answer:
Images are the single biggest performance lever for content-heavy apps. The two failures: loading a much larger image than you display (memory + decode time), and re-decoding the same image on every rebuild (CPU, GC).

Downsizing — the most important rule:
• `Image.network`/`Image.asset` accepts `cacheWidth`/`cacheHeight`. If your widget shows a 200×200 thumbnail, set `cacheWidth: 400` (2x for high-DPI). Without this, Flutter decodes the full 4000×4000 source into RAM.
• Pair with `BoxFit.cover` for clean display — the cached size is the decoded size, not the displayed size.

Caching:
• Default `ImageCache` keeps decoded bitmaps in memory. Limit it (`maximumSize`, `maximumSizeBytes`) to prevent uncontrolled growth on image-heavy screens.
• `cached_network_image` adds a disk cache so reopens are instant and offline-ish. Configure its size separately.
• ListView.builder of images: scrolling out and back in re-displays from cache; only first display pays decode cost.

Codecs and formats:
• Prefer WebP/AVIF over JPEG/PNG when your CDN supports it — same quality, 20–50% smaller, faster decode
• SVG via flutter_svg adds runtime cost; for large/complex SVGs, ship pre-rendered raster assets
• Lottie / Rive for animated assets — much cheaper than playing GIF/WebM in flutter

When rendering is still slow:
• Use `RepaintBoundary` to isolate the image so neighbor rebuilds don't repaint it
• `precacheImage` during page transitions — decode the next page's hero image while the current one is still on screen
• Display a low-res placeholder (frame_blur, BlurHash) until the full image is ready
• If your list is image-heavy, consider FadeInImage with a memory-cached placeholder

question: When does Opacity / BackdropFilter / shadows hurt, and how do you fix it?
answer:
Some Flutter widgets force expensive raster work — they look harmless but turn the GPU into the bottleneck. If your `--profile` shows raster bar > UI bar, this is where to look.

Opacity widget:
• Wraps its subtree in a saveLayer compositing operation — every pixel is painted into an offscreen buffer, then alpha-blended onto the parent
• Cost is roughly proportional to the painted area
• Fixes:
  - For text/icons, use `color.withOpacity(...)` directly (shader blend, cheap)
  - For images, use `Image.opacity` parameter (cheap)
  - For animation, use `FadeTransition` / `AnimatedOpacity` — they smartly disable saveLayer when alpha is 0 or 1

BackdropFilter (frosted glass):
• ImageFilter.blur creates a separate compositing pass — VERY expensive on the GPU, especially for large blur sigmas
• Fixes:
  - Use a smaller blurred area (only the affected region, not full screen)
  - Pre-render a static blurred snapshot when the underlying content rarely changes
  - Cache via RepaintBoundary so the blur isn't recomputed when nothing under it changed
  - Reduce blur sigma; use stylized colors instead of true blur

DropShadows on lots of items:
• Each shadow is a separate paint op; lists of shadowed cards multiply that cost per frame
• Fixes:
  - Hardcode the shadow into a 9-slice background image where possible
  - Use a single subtle border or a flat "tinted background" instead of shadow
  - For long lists, limit shadows to focused items (selected/hover) only

ClipPath / ClipRRect:
• Clips often force a saveLayer too, particularly with antialiasing on. Prefer `Clip.hardEdge` if visual quality allows; better, replace with a Container decorated with rounded BoxDecoration (no saveLayer required).

question: What's the difference between Skia and Impeller and how do shader compilation jank avoidance work?
answer:
Flutter has two rendering backends:

Skia — the original 2D engine (Chrome, Android). Mature, broad. On Android Skia uses runtime shader compilation: the first time it draws a particular blend/clip combination, it compiles the shader on the GPU thread, which can take 5–50ms — visible as a single dropped frame on first scroll/animation. Solution: SKSL warmup files captured during a scripted dry-run and shipped with the app, replayed at startup so all shaders are pre-compiled.

Impeller — Flutter's newer renderer:
• Default on iOS since 3.10
• Default on Android since 3.16 (with fallbacks for older devices)
• Uses precompiled shaders shipped with the engine — eliminates first-run shader jank entirely
• Better consistency across frames (less GC pressure on platform-thread allocations)
• Slight differences in pixel rendering vs Skia — usually invisible, but golden-image tests may need updates

What you should know:
• On modern Flutter, you usually don't need SKSL warmup — Impeller solves it. If you still see first-frame jank, check whether your build uses Impeller (`--enable-impeller` on Android, default on iOS).
• If you must support old Android devices that fall back to Skia, capture and ship SKSL warmups.
• Impeller has a few known regressions (custom shaders, rare BackdropFilter cases) — track engine release notes when upgrading Flutter SDK.

General shader/raster cost reduction tips also apply to both: avoid huge BlendMode pipelines, avoid shadows on hundreds of objects, prefer hard-edge clips.
code_example:
# Capture SKSL warmup (Skia only)
# 1) Run on a real device in --profile mode
flutter run --profile --cache-sksl --purge-persistent-cache
# 2) Exercise all the screens/animations that produced jank
# 3) Quit the app, then save the cache
flutter screenshot --type=skia --observatory-url=<observatory_url>  # not the right cmd, illustrative
# Real workflow uses 'flutter run --cache-sksl' followed by:
flutter build apk --bundle-sksl-path=flutter_01.sksl.json

# Verify Impeller is on (Android)
flutter run -d <android> --enable-impeller
# AndroidManifest <meta-data
#   android:name="io.flutter.embedding.android.EnableImpeller"
#   android:value="true"/>

# Inspect renderer at runtime
// import 'dart:ui' as ui;
// print(ui.PlatformDispatcher.instance.implicitView!.platformDispatcher.runtimeType);
// More commonly, check Flutter logs at startup — engine prints which backend is active.

# In CI: regression test that scrolls a complex page and asserts shader-warmup
# behaviour by counting frames > 16ms in the first 2 seconds of interaction.

question: How do you keep startup time low — splash, deferred work, deferred components?
answer:
Time-to-interactive on cold start is one of the most-watched mobile KPIs. Three layers contribute: native pre-Flutter initialization, Flutter engine warm-up, and your app's first-frame work. Each has its own knobs.

Native splash (pre-Flutter):
• Use `flutter_native_splash` to render a static image as the system splash. The OS shows it the instant the user taps the icon, before anything Flutter exists.
• Without it, Android shows a blank white window for ~300–800ms while the engine boots.

Main entry — keep it lean:
• Don't `await` IO in main() unless absolutely required. Hydrate prefs, theme, and other quick reads — but kick off heavier loads (auth refresh, feature flags fetch) AFTER `runApp` so the first frame paints first.
• Avoid synchronous plugin calls that hit a platform channel before runApp — they add tens of ms.
• Splash screen widget shows immediately, hydrates state, then transitions to the real screen.

Deferred initialization:
• Heavy services (analytics, crash reporter, websocket) — initialize on the first frame post-paint via `WidgetsBinding.instance.addPostFrameCallback`. The user sees the UI render before these add overhead.
• Lazy singletons (get_it / Riverpod providers) — initialized on first use, not on app start.
• Disk caches — initialize on demand, not eagerly.

Deferred components (Android only):
• `flutter build appbundle` supports split components — modules of your app that are NOT in the initial install but are downloaded on demand from Play Store
• Useful for occasional features (advanced editor, AR scanner) that bloat install size
• Not needed for most apps — but for big apps, can shave 30–50% off the install download

question: How do you build a long, smooth scrolling list without jank?
answer:
The performance gap between a hand-rolled list and a properly-built one is enormous. The wrong tool gets you 10fps at 1000 items; the right one gets you 60fps at 100k.

Must-haves:
• ListView.builder / ListView.separated — never `ListView(children: [...])` for any list bigger than what fits on screen. Builder lazily creates only visible widgets.
• Stable item heights when possible — `ListView.builder(itemExtent: 72, ...)` lets Flutter skip per-item layout. Even better: precise height enables instant jump-to-index on scroll.
• Use `Sliver`-based lists (CustomScrollView + SliverList) when you mix sections; they share a single Scrollable and render more efficiently than nested ListViews.

Item-level discipline:
• `const` constructors throughout the item subtree — Flutter skips identical const widgets entirely
• Avoid creating closures or new objects in itemBuilder — pre-extract handlers to fields/static
• Use `cacheExtent` to render slightly off-screen items — smoother scroll, slightly more memory
• Don't rebuild items on global state changes — use selector patterns so only the items whose data changed rebuild

Image discipline (lists are usually image-heavy):
• `cacheWidth`/`cacheHeight` — decode at the displayed size, not the source size
• `cached_network_image` for memory + disk cache
• `RepaintBoundary` around image tiles to isolate raster work

When scrolling 1000s+ items:
• Add a search/filter UI so the user doesn't have to scroll through all of them
• Paginate with `infinite_scroll_pagination` — fetch next page when the last item appears, not all up front
• Virtualization-related tools: `flutter_list_view` and other community alternatives if you need anchor-based jumps
